g.data-narration: Welcome to CPU Scheduling! Today we'll explore how operating systems efficiently manage processor time.
CPU scheduling is the foundation of multiprogramming. It allows multiple processes to share one CPU, maximizing utilization.
g.text: CPU scheduling is the basis of multiprogrammed OS. By switching the CPU among processes,
the OS maximizes CPU utilization. In a single-processor system, only one process runs at a time.

g.data-narration: Processes alternate between CPU bursts and I/O waits in a cycle. This alternating pattern is fundamental to scheduling.
Processes are either CPU-bound (compute-heavy) or I/O-bound (wait-heavy). Most bursts are short; few are long.
The CPU scheduler selects which process from the ready queue runs next. The ready queue can be any data structure.
Preemptive scheduling allows the OS to forcibly take the CPU away. Non-preemptive lets processes keep CPU until they yield.
g.text: Decisions only at: I/O wait or Termination
Process keeps CPU until it yields
OS can forcibly interrupt running process
⚠ Issue: Preemptive can cause race conditions on shared data

g.data-narration: Four circumstances trigger scheduling decisions. Two are non-preemptive; all four enable preemptive scheduling.
The dispatcher performs the actual context switch: save state, load new state, switch to user mode, jump to program.
g.text: → Should be as short as possible (pure overhead)

g.data-narration: Five criteria evaluate scheduling: CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround time, waiting time, and response time.
Master these formulas: TAT equals completion minus arrival, waiting equals TAT minus burst, utilization is total burst over total time.
Let's solve an example: three processes with FCFS. P1 runs first, then P2, then P3. Watch how waiting time accumulates.
To summarize: CPU scheduling maximizes utilization. The scheduler selects processes; the dispatcher performs context switches. Remember the formulas!
g.text: CPU scheduling enables multiprogramming → maximizes CPU utilization
CPU scheduler SELECTS process; dispatcher PERFORMS context switch
Preemptive = all 4 circumstances; Non-preemptive = only 1 and 4
TAT = CT - AT; WT = TAT - BT; Scheduling affects only waiting time
